Extract and structure the following information as valid JSON only (no markdown, no extra text).Return ONLY this JSON structure:{"summary": "2-3 sentence summary of the video content","keyPoints": ["point 1", "point 2", ...],"aiConcepts": ["AI concept 1", "AI concept 2", ...],"technicalDetails": ["tool/framework 1", "implementation detail", ...],"visualContextFlags": [{"timestamp": 120, "reason": "Code demo", "context": "Shows Python implementation"}]}If the video is not about AI/tech, set aiConcepts and technicalDetails to empty arrays but still extract keyPoints.TRANSCRIPT:Stay tuned because later in thisvideo, I'm going to show you how to take this starter input prompt. That's simply just a couple of sentences. And get today's framework to optimizeit into something That does full train of thought reasoning, traces, just likethese to improve your prompts massively. Okay. So one of the things that I'vementioned on the channel, time and time again is that the quality ofthe outputs you get from an LLM are, totally conditional of the contextand the inputs that you put into this. Now, this sounds really obvious yettime and time again, I see people making this mistake of not optimizing theirprompts of not thinking  about what kind of context should be going in withthe prompt of thinking about things like in context learning, et cetera. And that brings me to today'stool that I want to talk about, which is a new framework fromMicrosoft called PromptWizard. And the idea here with this framework isthat it's trying to help you optimize the prompt that you use for the instructions. What in context learningexamples, how many, et cetera? And overall, just what you putinto the model for a specific task. So you can imagine this is a reallyimportant thing for big companies like Microsoft, like Google, is that as they train up new models, infact, as they even finetune new models, often the prompts needed to get the bestresult out of that model will change. Now, this is something that many labshave worked on trying to standardize, so that just one simple prompt for a taskwould be able to get the best results out. Yet, so far, no one'sreally conquered this. And this leads to prompt engineering,still being a thing multiple years after it first came on the scene. Now the models in generalhave gotten a lot better. So therefore it's perhaps notas magical as it was before. And I think the education around promptengineering has gotten a lot better. So people are sort of learning howto set the context, how to give it some kind of identity, et cetera. But prompt with it approaches thiswhole thing as an optimization problem. And not only are they lookingat getting the best prompting to create a correct answer out. They're also interested in gettingthe models to be able to generate better chains of thought out. and synthetically generate, chainsof thought, which is exactly what you could imagine people wanting to doif they want to train up reasoning models where they've got both correctchains of thought and incorrect chains of thought that they can train on toget models better at generating out reasoning via chain of thought patterns. So let's jump in and have a look at this. So Microsoft clearly knows thatthis is a big problem at the moment with what they're working on. they really want to have, an optimizedway of doing this that's not just human trial and error, et cetera. and this is where they'reintroducing PromptWizard. So this framework is designed to automateand simplify prompt optimization. The idea here is that it can use amodel to get feedback, from LLMs. It can use a LLM as a judge to critiquethe outputs and then update the inputs going in for producing those outputs. And then over time, it's able toboth evolve those instructions. And also the in context learningexamples that are going in there. So they talk about threekey insights in here. The first being feedbackdriven refinements. So PromptWizard leverages aniterative feedback loop where the LLM generates critiques and refinesits own prompts and examples. This is at the simplest level. The next level is basically  where,it's doing joint optimization and starting to create synthetic examplesthat a diverse, but still in line with the tasks that you're trying to do. And then thirdly, and perhaps mostinterestingly is the whole idea of this self generated chain of thoughts steps. So this is incorporating chain ofthought reasoning to improve the problem-solving capabilities of the model. So if we have a look at this sortof diagram, we can see that you're going to input a problem description. You're going to input some kindof prompt instruction for that. So this is the questionthat you want to answer. Then you're going to have somekind of instruction for this. So you could think of this as being likea system prompt, a developer prompt, et cetera, is giving clear instructionsof how to handle the question above. And then you're going to havesome in-context learning, training examples in there. Now you can see that what thiswill do is basically refine those prompt instructions. so that they get passed alongand we can test out different modified prompt instructions. It will select diverse examplesfrom your training examples for the in context learning. and then be able to generatesynthetic examples out. And then finally it can generateout these sort of self-generated reasoning and validation. This is going to be through thechain of thought, et cetera. Until finally, we get aclear optimized prompt. some optimized few shot examples with thereasoning chain of thought, et cetera. And the sort of task intentand expert persona that we can use coming out of this. Okay. So if we look at this in a littlebit more detail, we can see that stage one of this is the refinementof the prompt instruction. So this prompt instruction that goes in. Obviously, you're going topass that in with the problem. In this case PromptWizard also passesin some sort of thinking styles. So this is a little bit similar tothe DeepMind prompt breeder idea of where you sort of mutate prompts. And you can see we get a set of newmutated instructions and stuff like that. these can then be scored tosee, okay, what went well? and we can, take each of these mutations. And see that okay this one came back out. So we can now critique and synthesizethat to improve the prompt instruction. And then you can go through. And again, in a loop as you're goingthrough this, because each time you're passing in a separate sort of pass,you don't have to worry too much about passing in the same problem descriptions,et cetera, going through this. So this point is doing thesort of mutation stuff based on these thinking styles. And then here we're doing all the sortof scoring and evaluation and getting feedback from the LLM, for this. Then once we've got those scores, wecan then basically critique it, why was this better, et cetera, and synthesizea new prompt that comes out even better. And the idea is this is a loop thatgoes through and you can choose how many times you want to go through theloop to improve the prompt instruction. The second stage is where you're nowcombining this with the in context, learning examples, et cetera. And so you're passing in these examples,you're critiquing and synthesizing. So this strengthened even more andallows us to basically get these better chain of thoughts for doingreasoning, et cetera at the end on this. All right. My focus is on the essential fields: summary, keyPoints, aiConcepts, technicalDetails, and visualContextFlags. I'm working to ensure the output is pure JSON.Adding Context and DetailsI've been thinking about the "aiConcepts" and "technicalDetails" arrays, filling them in as I delve deeper. I've noted "PromptWizard," a Microsoft framework for prompt optimization, using an iterative feedback loop. The framework uses synthetic examples and Chain of Thought (CoT). I also saw Python code in a Google Colab notebook, using the GSM8K dataset, OpenAI API/Azure, and YAML configs, transforming simple prompts into detailed expert personas.Refining Key ConceptsI am now focusing on refining the key points, concepts, and technical details. I've updated the "aiConcepts" to include In-context Learning (ICL) and evolutionary algorithms. The "technicalDetails" now include OpenAI's new models. This will allow for the data extraction to create more accurate and comprehensive JSON outputs.Analyzing Visual Context FlagsI'm now focusing on the visual context flags extracted from the video. I've noted specific timestamps. I'm taking the timestamps for the PromptWizard workflow, the Google Colab walkthrough, and the before/after prompt comparison, and logging them into the data. I've also noted that the code example uses YAML configuration files and is under the MIT License, which is important for the project context.Improving Prompt Generation TechniquesI am now delving into the "PromptWizard" system, noting its capabilities in prompt refinement, using joint optimization of examples, and self-generated Chain of Thought. I can see it transforms basic instructions into detailed expert personas with structured logic. I've also noted that the system outperforms human trial and error.Deepening Optimization StrategiesI'm now integrating an optimization stage into the process. This involves refining, mutating, and evaluating prompts, focusing on the underlying Python code demo in Google Colab that I extracted before. I have logged timestamps for the code demo and the prompt comparison. I am looking to ensure the JSON is valid and only includes the raw string, without any additional markdown or extra text as requested. Expand to view model thoughts  chevron_right

editmore_vert{"summary": "The video introduces PromptWizard, a Microsoft framework designed to automate and optimize LLM prompts through iterative feedback and synthetic reasoning. It demonstrates how to transform simple instructions into highly structured, expert-level prompts that use Chain of Thought reasoning to improve model performance.","keyPoints": ["LLM output quality is highly dependent on the quality of context and input instructions provided.","PromptWizard uses an 'LLM-as-a-judge' feedback loop to critique and refine prompts automatically.","The framework employs three key insights: feedback-driven refinement, joint optimization of instructions/examples, and self-generated Chain of Thought steps.","It simplifies the prompt engineering process by treating it as an optimization problem rather than human trial-and-error.","The tool can generate complex expert personas and structured problem-solving steps tailored to specific datasets like GSM8K."],"aiConcepts": ["Prompt Optimization","Chain of Thought (CoT)","In-Context Learning (ICL)","LLM-as-a-judge","Synthetic Data Generation","Prompt Mutation","Meta-prompting","Zero-shot and Few-shot learning"],"technicalDetails": ["Microsoft PromptWizard framework","GSM8K (Grade School Math) dataset","OpenAI GPT-4o / GPT-4o mini","MIT License","YAML configuration","Python / Google Colab implementation"],"visualContextFlags": [{"timestamp": 214,"reason": "Optimization Workflow","context": "Displays a diagram showing the stages of instruction refinement, example selection, and reasoning generation."},{"timestamp": 524,"reason": "Code implementation","context": "Shows a Google Colab notebook demonstrating how to set up PromptWizard, environment variables, and GSM8K data processing."},{"timestamp": 810,"reason": "Output comparison","context": "Visualizes the difference between a simple 'step-by-step' starter prompt and the final, highly structured optimized prompt."}]}thumb_upthumb_down
